era_context: The year -140 marks the consolidation of major power shifts across Eurasia. Emperor Wu of Han is consolidating personal authority in China, beginning his transformative reign. The Parthian Empire under Mithridates I is at the height of its expansion, challenging Seleucid dominance. Simon Maccabeus rules an independent Judea. Rome maintains Mediterranean dominance but faces no immediate rivals. The Hellenistic world is fragmenting under pressure from rising Eastern powers.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe and Scandinavia: minimal documentation
Mesoamerica and South America: no synchronization with Old World chronologies

events.title: Emperor Wu of Han consolidates personal authority
events.description: Emperor Wu of Han (r. -141 to -87) consolidates personal authority, gradually reducing the influence of the Empress Dowager Dou and other regents. By -140, Emperor Wu is firmly in control of the imperial government and begins implementing his own policies. This consolidation marks the beginning of Emperor Wu's transformative reign, which will see the expansion of Han territory, the establishment of the Silk Road, military campaigns against the Xiongnu, and the flourishing of Han culture and administration.

sources.name: Han Shu (Book of Han), Chapter 4 (Annals of Emperor Wu)
Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian), Chapter 12 (Annals of Emperor Wu)

events.certainty_note: Emperor Wu's consolidation of power is well-attested in Han sources, but the precise timing is uncertain. The process was gradual over several years, with -140 marking a conventional point of consolidation.

events.title: Han Dynasty establishes diplomatic missions to the west
events.region: China, Han Dynasty and Central Asia
events.description: Under Emperor Wu's direction, the Han Dynasty begins establishing diplomatic missions to the west, particularly to the Yuezhi confederation in Central Asia. These missions are motivated by the desire to form alliances against the Xiongnu and to establish trade routes. The most famous mission is that of Zhang Qian, who is sent to the Yuezhi around -138 but is captured by the Xiongnu and held for years. These early diplomatic efforts lay the groundwork for the later establishment of the Silk Road.
key_figures: Zhang Qian (diplomat and explorer, sent c. -138)

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian), Chapter 123 (Biography of Zhang Qian)
Han Shu (Book of Han), Chapter 61 (Biography of Zhang Qian)

events.certainty_note: Han diplomatic missions to the west are well-attested in Chinese sources. Zhang Qian's mission is traditionally dated to c. -138, but some scholars propose -140 or -139. The exact date is uncertain.
cross_references: Follows from -140-001 (Emperor Wu's consolidation of power)
Leads to -138-001 (Zhang Qian's mission and Silk Road establishment)

events.title: Mithridates I of Parthia at the height of his power
events.description: Mithridates I of Parthia (r. -171 to -138) is at the height of his power and territorial expansion. By -140, Parthia controls vast territories from Mesopotamia to the borders of India, making it one of the great powers of the ancient world. Parthia rivals Rome in the west and dominates the Near East. Mithridates I's reign represents the transformation of Parthia from a regional kingdom into a major imperial power.
events.certainty_note: Mithridates I's reign and territorial expansion are well-attested in numismatic evidence and later Greek and Roman sources. The date -140 marks a conventional point in his reign.
cross_references: Part of broader Parthian expansion (-171 to -138)

events.title: Simon Maccabeus expands Hasmonean territory
events.description: Simon Maccabeus (r. -142 to -135) continues expanding Hasmonean territory, conquering neighboring regions and fortifying cities. By -140, the Hasmonean kingdom has expanded beyond the traditional boundaries of Judea, incorporating parts of Samaria and other territories. Simon's military campaigns and territorial expansion establish the Hasmonean kingdom as a significant regional power.
key_figures: Simon Maccabeus (Jewish leader, high priest, ethnarch)
events.certainty_note: Simon's territorial expansion is attested in Jewish sources, but specific campaigns in -140 are not precisely dated.
cross_references: Follows from -141-004 (Simon's consolidation of Hasmonean rule)

events.title: Roman consolidation in the Mediterranean
events.description: Rome consolidates its dominance in the Mediterranean following the destruction of Carthage (-146) and Corinth (-146). Roman provinces are established in Greece, Macedonia, Asia Minor, and North Africa. Rome faces no significant rivals in the Mediterranean, but Eastern powers (Parthia, Han China) are rising. Roman expansion in the Iberian Peninsula continues, with ongoing campaigns against Lusitanian and Celtiberian peoples.
key_figures: Roman Senate and magistrates (names uncertain for this specific year)
events.certainty_note: Roman consolidation in the Mediterranean is well-attested, but specific events in -140 are not precisely documented.
cross_references: Follows from -146-001 (destruction of Corinth and Carthage)
Part of broader Roman expansion in the Mediterranean

disconfirming_evidence: The date of Emperor Wu's consolidation of personal authority is uncertain; some scholars place it earlier (-141) or later (-139). Zhang Qian's mission is traditionally dated to c. -138, but some sources suggest -140 or -139. The significance of Han diplomatic missions in -140 specifically is debated; these were gradual processes rather than discrete events. Mithridates I's reign is well-attested, but the precise extent of his territorial control in -140 is uncertain. Simon Maccabeus's territorial expansion is attested, but the pace and extent of expansion in -140 specifically are not precisely documented.
historiographic_note: Documentation for -140 is rich for China and moderate for Judea and the Near East. Han Dynasty records are detailed and reliable, compiled within centuries of the events. Jewish sources (1 Maccabees) provide accounts of Simon's rule, but these were written after the events. Parthian history relies on later Greek and Roman sources and numismatic evidence. Roman sources for this period are fragmentary. The Seleucid Empire is poorly documented in surviving sources. Sub-Saharan Africa, Northern Europe, and the Americas remain undocumented.

graph_edges.note: Emperor Wu's consolidation of power enabled diplomatic missions to the west
Early diplomatic missions led to Zhang Qian's mission and Silk Road establishment
Mithridates I's power at its height was followed by his death in -138
Simon's territorial expansion was interrupted by his assassination
Roman consolidation continued with ongoing campaigns in Iberia
